policy_statement: Build additional desalination capacity: Kuwait produced around 3.85 MCM/d of desalinated water in 2016; eight additional plants were being commissioned and additional IWPP/plant projects are planned, including Al-Zour North (250 MGD), Al-Zour South (145 MGD), Al Khiran (125 MGD), and Doha (60 MGD), with further projects planned to be tendered as of 2019 (~285 MGD additional).
mechanism_description: Expanding desalination plant capacity increases reliable freshwater production, improving water supply resilience under climate-driven scarcity.
typology_evidence_quote: "Eight additional plants were being commissioned by the government... with an additional capacity of around 285 MGD are planned to be tendered"
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/variable precipitation/sea-level salinity intrusion) → ↑ reliable water supply capacity → ↓ water shortage risk / ↓ exposure & sensitivity
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Governments/utilities expand desalination infrastructure (new plants, capacity uprates, intake/outfall works, associated transmission/storage) to increase baseline and peak freshwater production.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reverse osmosis/thermal desalination converts saline/brackish water to freshwater, decoupling potable supply from rainfall, river flow, and reservoir inflows (and can offset salinity intrusion impacts on coastal aquifers).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Improved continuity and reliability of municipal/industrial water supply during hotter/drier periods and multi-year droughts, reducing socioeconomic disruption and health risks from water scarcity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced vulnerability to climate-driven water scarcity by increasing reliable potable water supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism increases a drought-resilient water source to reduce water shortage risk under climate variability and change (hazard → reduced exposure/sensitivity).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While desalination materially affects energy use and thus GHG emissions, the intent and largest climate-relevant effect of “capacity expansion for resilience” is maintaining water availability under scarcity—an adaptation outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: If powered by renewables or coupled with efficiency, there can be mitigation co-benefits; if powered by fossil electricity, there can be net emissions increases—these do not change that the dominant pathway is service resilience to climate stress.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is “expand desalination capacity to improve reliable supply under scarcity” functions through supply augmentation and reduced shortage vulnerability, i.e., adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., maladaptation risk, emissions-intensive adaptation, nature impacts, renewable-powered desalination) requires policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Desalination can be emissions-intensive and environmentally disruptive (brine discharge, coastal ecosystem impacts); some implementations may increase GHGs (mitigation negative) even though the primary climate function remains adaptation (water supply resilience). If a policy’s stated primary purpose is decarbonization via renewable-powered desalination, it could warrant different categorization, but that is outside this canonical mechanism as defined.
key_indicators: “Building desalination plants”, “Eight additional plants were being commissioned”, “IWPP”, “planned to be tendered”, “additional capacity”, “capacity: 250 MGD…145 MGD…125 MGD…60 MGD…285 MGD”
co_benefits: Energy/industrial development and grid investment associated with IWPPs; potential public health benefits from improved water quality/reliability. (Not explicitly framed as primary objectives in the text.)
instance_edge_case_notes: IWPP/tendering language could also indicate a procurement/PPP instrument, but the core action in the text is the physical expansion of desalination capacity, so it is classified as infrastructure investment.
